This is interesting. Certainly a "weakness" of alphabetic scripts are their inefficient use of character-wise-space compared to logographic ones(see Japanese twitter vs. English twitter) so compensating for that by relaxing the linearity constraint at the word-level is a neat way to get around that.

From the description it's not super clear to me if this is a "serious" or a "fun" conlang tho(or maybe that's a false distinction!). The claims in uscript.pdf do seem a bit grandoise tho...

and master of editors Marijn Havebeke talking about programs as a series of immutable states comes very close to my aspirations & desires,
<https://mobile.twitter.com/MarijnJH/status/1191720489523318786>

attachments.fallback: <https://twitter.com/MarijnJH|@MarijnJH>: Are there any incremental computation systems that treat inputs not as mutable cells, but model the whole system as a persistent data structure, and updates create a new, structure-sharing instance?

text: kind of off topic, but this makes me think of the article from august about Twain & his interest & investment in pocket watches, in that Twain seems really to have been a pretty advanced fellow, <https://www.smithsonianmag.com/history/mark-twains-quest-bring-affordable-watches-masses-180972813/>

text:
hmm I haven't dug into it yet myself, but I ran across this idea called "differential dataflow" that seems to be using the right words. I think one of the contributors to this implementatiion was experimenting with extending it to a React-js like system for transparently generating DOM:

<https://github.com/TimelyDataflow/differential-dataflow>

hmm I haven't dug into it yet myself, but I ran across this idea called "differential dataflow" that seems to be using the right words. I think one of the contributors to this implementatiion was experimenting with extending it to a React-js like system for transparently generating DOM:

<https://github.com/TimelyDataflow/differential-dataflow>

Differential dataflow is an iterative, incremental, stream processor basically. The rust version of Eve was based on taking the main idea behind differential dataflow and specializing it for a datalog-ish semantics. It worked very well, though implementing it is extremely unintuitive.

It's really great work. Frank McSherry (the guy who started it) is one of my absolute favorite people in the space. He's wonderfully irreverent :slightly_smiling_face:

text: hey! i think you got interviewed on some podcast i listened to! welcome!

here are a few other things about excel that i don’t see talked about very often: 

1. started with existing conceptual tools (double entry accounting, arithmetic, graphs and charts) and gradually extended them, rather than forcing early adopters to immediately understand a whole new abstraction before they could get anything done
2. direct editing: you don’t have to issue commands to get data entry done. you don’t have to click a link to get to a different part of a sheet. you just click on cells to edit them. if you want abstractions you learn more complex menus and commands but (given enough time and patience) you could manually do pretty much all of excel with the basics. this also makes learning easier
3. saved state: people copy scratch info to random areas of their tables ALL THE TIME and it’s an incredibly rare feature. wish i had it for the browser
4. your data is never “finished”. every spreadsheet is a breathing document. they get updated all the time and work in almost every intermediate state. it’s data transformation with structurally typed holes
5. ubiquitous metaphor. everything is a table
6. types are visual. you know that blank fields will give you different results. you can see which columns have them


just off the top of my head
<@UHWC9PXBL> oh snap! you listened to the podcast! thanks!

I've been pretty quiet lately because I've had my head down working on WhiteBox, a tool to show you how your (compiled) code behaves as you write it (similar to Glen Chiacchieri's REPLugger, if you saw that).
I'd like some feedback on what people would like to see more of. I'm thinking about doing a livestream presentation this weekend... I might do some UI mockups to give you an idea of what I'm aiming for (and to get some criticism)
Apologies for the silence, I wanted to get something up quickly... hopefully it makes sense.
EDIT: video replaced with a version with commentary below

attachments.title: WhiteBox (REPL/live debugger) demonstration 1 - live reloading and variable values
attachments.fallback: YouTube Video: WhiteBox (REPL/live debugger) demonstration 1 - live reloading and variable values
